content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901         322-6441        322-6444

August 6, 1979

Ms. Joy Anne Fitzhugh
Post Office Box 434
Cambria, CA 93428

Dear Ms. Fitzhugh:

This is in response to your letter of July 20, 1979, requesting advice from us concerning conflict of interest problems which might be raised with respect to your seat on the South Central Coast Regional Commission. Because you are not asking about your participation in a particular decision facing you but are instead asking for broad guidelines, this advice is not provided pursuant to Section 83114 and carries none of the immunizing effects of that section.1/ If you are, in the future, faced with a decision which you believe may raise problems under the Act, you may seek advice from us under Section 83114 at that time and we will be happy to supply it in the particular context created by the decision. The facts as I understand them from your letter and from our telephone conversation of June 20, 1979, are as follows.

You are the Treasurer of the San Luis Obispo County Farm Bureau and the Legislative Chairman of the San Luis Obispo County Cattlemen's Association and the San Luis Obispo County Cowbelles' Association. All three of these are non-profit associations and you do not receive any salary or reimbursement for expenses from any of them. In addition, in partnership with your parents, you operate a cattle grazing business on a 2000 acre tract of land. 32 acres, all owned by your father, are within the Coastal Zone. Finally, you have an investment in three companies owned by Standard Oil Company. You have asked what problems these circumstances raise under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

_________________________

1/ All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise provided.

Ms. Joy Anne Fitzhugh
August 6, 1979                                                Page Two


     The portion of the Political Reform Act that restricts the
actions of public officials in situations where conflicts of
interest exist is Section 87100, which provides that:

          No public official at any level of state or
     local government shall make, participate in making or
     in any way attempt to use his official position or
     influence a decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest.

     Section 87103 expands this idea to provide that:

          An official has a financial interest in a
     decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
     it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
     will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
     able from its effect on the public generally, on:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

          (b)  Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

          (c)  Any source of income, other than loans
     by a commercial lending institution in the regular
     course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
     promised to the public official within twelve
     months prior to the time when the decision is made;
     or

          (d)  Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.

     In addition, the term "business entity" as used in the Act
is defined in Section 82005 to mean "any organization or enterprise
operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship,
partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate,
corporation or association."  [Emphasis added.]

     The first thing to note about the operation of these sections
is that they do not in any way restrict what you may do in your
private capacity.  Given your private financial interests, they
restrict what you can do in your public capacity.  Secondly, as

Ms. Joy Anne Fitzhugh
August 6, 1979                                                Page Three


neither the Farm Bureau, the Cattlemen's Association or the
Cowbelles' Association is operated for profit, they are not
business entities, and the Political Reform Act does not require
you to disqualify yourself from decisions which might affect
them unless you received more than $250 in income of some sort
from them within twelve months prior to the time the decision
is made.

     You do not say in your letter how much stock you have in
the three companies owned by Standard Oil.  Assuming that your
investments in them are worth more than $1,000, these invest-
ments and your agricultural partnership may in some cases require
you to disqualify yourself.  The question of when such disqual-
ification is required is one which can be answered only in the
context of the individual decisions you will be facing.  The
standard to be applied in each case is whether it is foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
any of these investments.  Such effects may occur not only when
the entity in which you have an investment is a party to a
particular proceeding before the Commission, but also when the
Commission is making a decision which will have an indirect
effect on entities which are not parties, either because of the
precedential effects of the decision or otherwise.

     In closing, let me stress again that the disqualification
provisions of the Act must be applied on a case by case basis,
and that if you have any doubt about your participation in any
particular decision, you should consult the Coastal Commission's
counsel or the Fair Political Practices Commission for guidance.
Finally, these comments extend only to the application of the
Political Reform Act.  For an opinion regarding other conflict
of interest laws, you must contact the Coastal Commission's
counsel or a private attorney.

                                                    Sincerely,

                                                    [signature]
                                                    Sarah Cameron
                                                    Counsel
                                                    Conflicts of Interest Division

STC:mfa

79124

P. O. Box 434
Cambria, Ca.
93428
July 20, 1979

Ms. Sara Cameron
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K St.
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Ms. Cameron,

Based upon a telephone conversation I had with you on
June 20, 1979, I am sending you this letter regarding the
conditions of conflict of interest re: my May appointment
to the South Central Coast Regional Commission. Prior to
my appointment to the Commission I had been active in a number
of organizations related to my field of agriculture. I would
appreciate it if you would be able to send me a letter stating
the opinion and position of the FPPC regarding my continuation
in these organizations and activities.

I currently hold the position of San Luis Obispo County
Farm Bureau Treasurer (an office which will expire in October
to be replaced by a term as County Secretary). This is an
unpaid position, where I actually do not handle any funds,
but sit on the county executive committee and the board of
directors for the county.

For the last 3 years I have been appointed to the position
of San Luis Obispo County Cowbelle Legislative Chairman and
commencing in 1979 San Luis Obispo County Cattlemen's Legislative
Alert Chairman. These two positions are inter-related. They
are unpaid positions. Commencing at my appointment to the
Coastal Commission I have not been reimbursed for any of
the activities I have undertaken in these two chairmanships,
and do not plan to handle any moneys in these positions in
the future. The organizations will be furnishing my materials
needed for mailing out legislative letters inside the direction
of the organizations. My main activity as Legislative Chairman
is to keep both organizations updated on local, state and
national legislative issues and to mail the county memberships
letters requesting action as I receive directives from the
state or county organizations. I sit on the boards of both
organizations in this capacity.

Sara Cameron                    -2-


     Based upon the information I am able to give you, I
hope that you will be able to answer the following questions
that have surfaced since my Coastal appointment.

     1.     It it permissable to remain in the capacity of
Treasurer and to continue on to Secretary of the San Luis
Obispo County Farm Bureau, and to remain as Legislative
Chairman for the San Luis Obispo County Cowbelles and
Cattlemen?

     2.     What is the conflict if a Coastal related issue
arises in these organizations and how should such an issue
be handled (especially as legislative chairman)?

     3.     Can I as a private citizen voice my opinions regarding
Coastal matters, both in these organizations and as I give
speeches and in talking to individuals?  Where does the liability
lie or extend?

     4.     Based upon my appointment to the Commission, can
I as an individual support issues and candidates of local,
state, and national importance and what is the advisability
of entering into such activities and what are the liabilities?

     5.     What is, if any, the conflict of discussing the
Coastal Act, and laws as they are written? (ie. informing the
public as to what the law reads wither in person or by the
newspapers)?  Where would the liability be ?

     6.     As I belong to a family partnership (informal as
no partnership papers have ever been drawn up) with my parents
(partners) owning land in the coastal zone that we operate
(approximately 32 acres of a 2000 acre operation), I need to
know where my conflict of interest exists when voting on
coastal agricultural issues.  Does it apply to issues on
neighboring property, or does it extend to any agricultural
operation in the coastal Zone regardless of the location?
Does the similarity of the operation in the zone have a
relevance or would a conflict exist regardless of the similarity
of the operations (ie. any other beef operation - even though
our business is only in the cow-calf production)?

     7.     Because I own preferred and common stock in three
(3) Standard Oil Companies, I realize that I will be unable
to vote on issues these three companies bring before the
Commission, but will this stock ownership create a conflict
of interest when I vote on issues from other oil companies?
(ie. other Standard Oil Companies or non-Standard Oil Companies?)

Sara Cameron                    -3-


     I do need the answers to each of these questions as
they are of vital importance to the organizations i belong
to and to my ability to function as a Commissioner.  I
would appreciate it if you could send these answers and
statement to me by August 15, 1979.  The organizations are
now forming their 1980 nominations for their offerships and
need the answers to all of these problems.


                         Thank you,
                         [signature]
                         Joy Anne Fitzhugh

P.O. Box 434
Cambria, Ca.
93428
July 20, 1979

Ms. Sara Cameron
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K St.
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

     7.  Because I own preferred and common stock in three

Sara Cameron                    -3-

                         Thank you,

                         [signature]
                         Joy Anne Fitzhugh

sections.question_synthetic: An appointee to the South Central Coast Regional Commission seeks FPPC guidance on conflict of interest issues related to her continued involvement in agricultural organizations (Farm Bureau, Cowbelles, Cattlemen), her family's land holdings in the coastal zone, her Standard Oil Company stock ownership, and her ability to engage in political activities and voice opinions on Coastal Act matters.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the public to FPPC, not an FPPC response. The letter contains detailed background information followed by seven numbered questions. No CONCLUSION or FPPC response section is present in this document. Letter dated July 20, 1979, requesting response by August 15, 1979.

embedding.summary: Joy Anne Fitzhugh, a newly appointed commissioner to the South Central Coast Regional Commission, requests FPPC advice on seven specific conflict of interest questions regarding her continued participation in agricultural organizations, family farm ownership in the coastal zone, stock ownership in Standard Oil companies, and her ability to engage in political activities and public advocacy.
